metadata.title: Private Pilot Certificate Curriculum
metadata.description: Comprehensive training curriculum for Private Pilot License (PPL) based on FAA Part 61 requirements
metadata.author: Example CFI

studyAreas.name: Aerodynamics and Aircraft Performance
studyAreas.description: Fundamental principles of flight and aircraft performance

studyAreas.name: Navigation and Flight Planning
studyAreas.description: Charts, navigation techniques, and cross-country planning

studyItems.name: Four Forces of Flight
studyItems.description: Understanding lift, weight, thrust, and drag, and their interactions during various phases of flight
studyItems.evaluationCriteria: Student can explain each force, their relationships, and how they affect aircraft in straight-and-level, climbing, descending, and turning flight

studyItems.name: Slow Flight and Stalls
studyItems.description: Recognition of stall indications, stall recovery procedures, and maneuvering during slow flight
studyItems.evaluationCriteria: Ground: Explains aerodynamics of stalls and recovery procedures. Flight: Demonstrates proper slow flight technique and stall recovery with minimal altitude loss

studyItems.name: Engine Operation
studyItems.description: Four-stroke engine cycle, engine components, and normal/abnormal indications
studyItems.evaluationCriteria: Student can describe engine operation, interpret engine instruments, and explain proper operating procedures

studyItems.name: Pilotage and Dead Reckoning
studyItems.description: Visual navigation techniques using landmarks and calculated navigation
studyItems.evaluationCriteria: Ground: Plans a cross-country using pilotage and DR. Flight: Maintains course using visual checkpoints and time/distance calculations

lessonPlans.title: Introduction to Aerodynamics
lessonPlans.motivation: Understanding how an airplane flies is fundamental to becoming a safe and competent pilot. This knowledge helps you make better decisions and handle unusual situations.
objectives: Explain the four forces acting on an aircraft
Describe how control surfaces affect flight
Understand the relationship between angle of attack and lift
lessonPlans.planDescription: Ground lesson introducing basic aerodynamic principles using models and diagrams. Discussion includes practical applications during normal flight operations.
lessonPlans.preStudyHomework: Read PHAK Chapter 5 (Aerodynamics of Flight) pages 5-1 through 5-20

lessonPlans.title: Slow Flight and Stall Practice
lessonPlans.motivation: Recognizing and recovering from stalls is critical for flight safety. Most stall/spin accidents occur during takeoff and landing phases.
objectives: Maintain coordinated flight during slow flight
lessonPlans.planDescription: After ground briefing on stall aerodynamics, practice slow flight at various configurations, followed by power-off and power-on stalls. Emphasis on coordination and timely recognition.
lessonPlans.preStudyHomework: Review stall recovery procedures in POH. Watch FAA stall/spin awareness video.

lessonPlans.title: Cross-Country Planning
lessonPlans.motivation: Cross-country flying opens up the practical utility of an airplane and is required for your certificate. Proper planning ensures safe and efficient flights.
objectives: Plan a cross-country flight using current charts
Calculate fuel requirements and weight & balance
lessonPlans.planDescription: Work together to plan an actual cross-country flight. Student will complete all planning elements including weather briefing, performance calculations, and navigation log.
lessonPlans.preStudyHomework: Obtain current sectional chart. Review PHAK Chapter 16 (Navigation).
